---
title: "H-2A Job Order Text Analysis"
subtitle: "Task Descriptions, Emerging Terms, and Verb-Object Pairs"
author: "Fernando Brito-González"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    toc_depth: 3
    theme: flatly
    highlight: tango
    number_sections: true
    fig_caption: true
    code_folding: hide
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo      = TRUE,
  warning   = FALSE,
  message   = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center"
)

mydir  <- "C:/Users/Fer/UF Dropbox/Fernando Jose Brito-Gonzalez/Fernando_Brito_Dissertation/h2a_labor"
out_dir <- paste0(mydir, "/h2a_labor_demand/processed")

library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
```

---

# Overview

This document describes the text analysis pipeline applied to H-2A temporary agricultural
worker job order descriptions filed with the U.S. Department of Labor (DOL). The analysis
covers job orders submitted between **2019 and 2025**, extracted from DOL's seasonal jobs
data feed (<https://seasonaljobs.dol.gov/>).

The pipeline has three stages:

| Stage | Script | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `unzip_jo.R` | Parse JSON job orders → extract task text |
| 2 | `text_analytics_jo.R` | N-gram frequency, TF-IDF, emerging/declining terms |
| 3 | `extract_tasks_jo.R` | Verb-object pairs from task descriptions |

---

# Data

## Source

H-2A job order data are available as structured JSON files from DOL's seasonal jobs feed.
Each file corresponds to a filing period and contains one record per job order (ETA-790A form).
The relevant text field is **Section A.8a** (`addmcSectionDetails`), which employers use to
describe the specific duties workers will perform.

## Parsing

```{r load-data, eval=FALSE}
# unzip_jo.R — core parsing logic
library(jsonlite)

path   <- paste0(mydir, "/data/jo/")
files  <- dir(path, pattern = "_jo.json")

df.text <- data.frame()
for (i in seq_along(files)) {
  h2a.json <- fromJSON(paste0(path, files[i]), simplifyDataFrame = FALSE)
  h2a.json <- lapply(h2a.json, json.tr)

  df0 <- do.call(bind_rows, lapply(h2a.json, json.df, "termsAndConditions")) %>%
    bind_rows(do.call(bind_rows, lapply(h2a.json, json.df, "jobFrontTextFields"))) %>%
    filter(!duplicated(.), addmcSectionNumber == "A.8a") %>%
    group_by(caseNumber) %>%
    summarise(addmcSectionDetails = paste0(unique(addmcSectionDetails), collapse = "; "),
              .groups = "drop")

  df.text <- bind_rows(df.text, df0)
}

saveRDS(df.text, file = paste0(out_dir, "/df_text_jo.rds"))
```

## Corpus Summary

```{r corpus-summary}
df <- readRDS(paste0(out_dir, "/df_text_jo.rds")) %>%
  filter(!is.na(addmcSectionDetails), addmcSectionDetails != "",
         dateSubmitted >= as.Date("2019-01-01")) %>%
  mutate(year    = lubridate::year(dateSubmitted),
         quarter = lubridate::quarter(dateSubmitted),
         period  = paste0(year, "-Q", quarter),
         n_words = str_count(addmcSectionDetails, "\\S+"))

tibble(
  Statistic = c("Job orders", "Date range", "Mean words per order",
                "Median words per order", "Total words"),
  Value = c(
    format(nrow(df), big.mark = ","),
    paste(min(df$dateSubmitted), "to", max(df$dateSubmitted)),
    round(mean(df$n_words)),
    round(median(df$n_words)),
    format(sum(df$n_words), big.mark = ",")
  )
) %>%
  kbl(caption = "Corpus Summary Statistics") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"), full_width = FALSE)
```

```{r docs-by-year}
df %>%
  count(year) %>%
  ggplot(aes(factor(year), n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2c7bb6") +
  geom_text(aes(label = format(n, big.mark = ",")), vjust = -0.4, size = 3.5) +
  labs(title = "H-2A Job Orders by Year",
       x = NULL, y = "Number of job orders") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

---

# Text Analytics

## Boilerplate Removal

Job order text in Section A.8a mixes two types of content:

- **Task descriptions** — employer-specific duties (what the worker will do)
- **Terms & Conditions (T&C)** — legal boilerplate copied verbatim across filings

To isolate task-relevant language, any sentence appearing in more than **3% of job orders**
is classified as boilerplate and removed before analysis.

```{r boilerplate, eval=FALSE}
BOILER_THRESH <- 0.03

sent_df <- df %>%
  select(caseNumber, addmcSectionDetails) %>%
  mutate(sentences = tokenizers::tokenize_sentences(addmcSectionDetails)) %>%
  unnest(sentences) %>%
  mutate(sent_norm = str_squish(str_to_lower(str_remove_all(sentences, "[^a-z\\s]"))))

boilerplate <- sent_df %>%
  distinct(caseNumber, sent_norm) %>%
  count(sent_norm) %>%
  filter(n > BOILER_THRESH * nrow(df)) %>%
  pull(sent_norm)
# 171 boilerplate sentences identified
```

After removal, **171 boilerplate sentences** were identified and excluded, leaving
98,929 documents with substantive task text.

## Stopword Filtering

Three layers of stopwords are applied during tokenization:

1. **Standard English** stopwords (`tidytext::stop_words`)
2. **Spanish** stopwords (`tidytext::get_stopwords(language = "es")`) — some filings
   include Spanish-language T&C text (e.g., *empleador*, *trabajador*, *contrato*)
3. **Domain-specific** terms that are ubiquitous but uninformative (e.g., *worker*,
   *employer*, *equipment*, *maintenance*)

## Top N-Grams

```{r res-load}
res <- readRDS(paste0(out_dir, "/text_analytics_jo.rds"))
```

### Unigrams

```{r top-uni-tbl}
res$top_uni %>%
  slice_head(n = 20) %>%
  kbl(caption = "Top 20 Unigrams") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
```

```{r plot-top-uni, fig.cap="Top 20 unigrams across all H-2A job orders."}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(out_dir, "/plot_top_unigrams.png"))
```

### Bigrams

```{r top-bi-tbl}
res$top_bi %>%
  slice_head(n = 20) %>%
  kbl(caption = "Top 20 Bigrams") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
```

```{r plot-top-bi, fig.cap="Top 20 bigrams across all H-2A job orders."}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(out_dir, "/plot_top_bigrams.png"))
```

### Trigrams

```{r top-tri-tbl}
res$top_tri %>%
  slice_head(n = 20) %>%
  kbl(caption = "Top 20 Trigrams") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
```

```{r plot-top-tri, fig.cap="Top 20 trigrams across all H-2A job orders."}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(out_dir, "/plot_top_trigrams.png"))
```

## Emerging and Declining Terms

To detect language change over time, the corpus is split chronologically into two
equal halves by document count:

- **Early period**: first 50,317 job orders
- **Late period**: last 50,316 job orders

For each n-gram, we compute:

$$\text{growth} = \frac{s_{\text{late}} - s_{\text{early}}}{s_{\text{early}} + \varepsilon}$$

where $s$ is the share of job orders containing the term in each half and
$\varepsilon = 10^{-9}$ prevents division by zero. Terms must appear in at least
0.5% of documents in each half to be included.

Points **above** the diagonal are emerging (more common recently); points **below**
are declining.

### Unigrams

```{r plot-emerge-uni, fig.cap="Emerging vs declining unigrams. Green = emerging; red = declining. Bubble size = absolute lift in share."}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(out_dir, "/plot_emerging_unigrams.png"))
```

### Bigrams

```{r plot-emerge-bi, fig.cap="Emerging vs declining bigrams (top 25 emerging, bottom 15 declining)."}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(out_dir, "/plot_emerging_bigrams.png"))
```

```{r top-emerging-bi}
res$emerging_bi %>%
  slice_head(n = 15) %>%
  select(bigram, share_early, share_late, growth, abs_lift) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(., 4))) %>%
  kbl(caption = "Top 15 Emerging Bigrams") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
```

```{r top-declining-bi}
res$emerging_bi %>%
  slice_tail(n = 15) %>%
  select(bigram, share_early, share_late, growth, abs_lift) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(., 4))) %>%
  arrange(growth) %>%
  kbl(caption = "Top 15 Declining Bigrams") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
```

### Trigrams

```{r plot-emerge-tri, fig.cap="Emerging vs declining trigrams (top 25 emerging, bottom 15 declining)."}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(out_dir, "/plot_emerging_trigrams.png"))
```

```{r top-emerging-tri}
res$emerging_tri %>%
  slice_head(n = 15) %>%
  select(trigram, share_early, share_late, growth, abs_lift) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(., 4))) %>%
  kbl(caption = "Top 15 Emerging Trigrams") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
```

## TF-IDF by Quarter

TF-IDF (term frequency–inverse document frequency) identifies terms that are
especially distinctive to a given quarter relative to all other quarters.
Each quarter is treated as a single "document" aggregating all job orders filed
in that period.

```{r plot-tfidf, fig.cap="Highest TF-IDF unigrams by quarter. Terms are distinctive to that quarter relative to all others.", fig.height=14}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(out_dir, "/plot_tfidf_by_period.png"))
```

---

# Task Verb Extraction

## Motivation

Standard n-gram analysis captures co-occurrence patterns but does not distinguish
**task verbs** (what workers do) from contextual nouns or modifiers. To characterize
labor demand in terms of concrete tasks, we extract **verb–object pairs** directly
from the task description text.

## Method: Regex-Based Extraction

Rather than full dependency parsing (which requires loading an NLP model for each
document), we use two complementary regex patterns that cover the dominant grammatical
structures in job description text:

**Gerund constructions** — the most common form in H-2A listings:

> *"harvesting grain crops, operating farm equipment, maintaining irrigation systems"*

```
([a-z]+ing)\s+((?:[a-z]+-?[a-z]*\s+){0,1}[a-z]+-?[a-z]*)
```

**Infinitive constructions**:

> *"to harvest corn, to operate a tractor, to maintain equipment"*

```
\bto\s+(<ag_verb>)\s+((?:[a-z]+-?[a-z]*\s+){0,1}[a-z]+-?[a-z]*)
```

## Agricultural Verb Filter

Extracted verbs are matched against a curated list of **agricultural task verbs**
(base forms). This filters out gerunds that happen to appear in legal boilerplate
that survived sentence-level removal (e.g., *"including"*, *"following"*,
*"understanding"*).

```{r ag-verbs}
ag_verbs <- c(
  "harvest","plant","cultivate","till","plow","irrigate","fertilize",
  "spray","apply","sow","seed","prune","thin","weed","mow","bale",
  "rake","disc","transplant","pollinate","fumigate","mulch","bed",
  "pick","pack","sort","grade","clip","trim","cut","bunch","wrap",
  "load","unload","haul","transport","drive","operate","handle",
  "feed","water","milk","shear","brand","vaccinate","castrate",
  "tag","weigh","pen","herd","move","rotate","breed","gather",
  "maintain","repair","clean","inspect","calibrate","adjust",
  "install","remove","replace","check","service","lubricate",
  "mix","prepare","store","stack","transfer","assemble",
  "wash","cool","dry","box","crate","label","count","measure",
  "record","monitor","observe","spread","dig"
)

tibble(`Agricultural task verbs (base form)` = sort(unique(ag_verbs))) %>%
  kbl() %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE) %>%
  column_spec(1, width = "15em")
```

## Results

```{r load-tasks}
tasks <- readRDS(paste0(out_dir, "/tasks_jo.rds"))
```

```{r task-summary}
tibble(
  Statistic = c("Verb-object pairs (total)", "Unique verbs", "Unique objects",
                "Job orders with at least one pair"),
  Value = c(
    format(nrow(tasks$verb_obj), big.mark = ","),
    format(n_distinct(tasks$verb_obj$verb_base), big.mark = ","),
    format(n_distinct(tasks$verb_obj$obj_lemma), big.mark = ","),
    format(n_distinct(tasks$verb_obj$caseNumber), big.mark = ",")
  )
) %>%
  kbl(caption = "Verb-Object Extraction Summary") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
```

### Top Task Verbs

```{r top-verbs-tbl}
tasks$top_verbs %>%
  slice_head(n = 20) %>%
  kbl(caption = "Top 20 Task Verbs") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
```

### Top Verb-Object Pairs

```{r top-vo-tbl}
tasks$top_vo %>%
  slice_head(n = 30) %>%
  select(pair, n) %>%
  kbl(caption = "Top 30 Verb-Object Pairs") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
```

```{r plot-vo, fig.cap="Top 30 verb-object pairs extracted from H-2A task descriptions."}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(out_dir, "/plot_verb_obj_top30.png"))
```

### Objects by Verb

```{r plot-facet, fig.cap="Top 6 objects per top-10 task verb.", fig.height=12}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(out_dir, "/plot_verb_facet_objects.png"))
```

### Verb Share Over Time

```{r plot-trends, fig.cap="Share of each task verb (among all verb tokens) by year, for the top 12 verbs."}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(out_dir, "/plot_verb_trends.png"))
```

```{r verb-year-tbl}
tasks$verb_year %>%
  filter(verb_base %in% tasks$top_verbs$verb_base[1:12]) %>%
  select(year, verb_base, n, share) %>%
  mutate(share = round(share * 100, 2)) %>%
  rename(pct_share = share) %>%
  arrange(verb_base, year) %>%
  kbl(caption = "Verb share by year — top 12 task verbs (share = % of all verb tokens)") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE) %>%
  scroll_box(height = "400px")
```

---

# Summary

The analysis reveals several patterns in H-2A labor demand over 2019–2025:

1. **Task vocabulary is stable**: the dominant task verbs (harvest, operate, maintain,
   plant, irrigate) reflect the permanent core of seasonal agricultural work.

2. **Emerging terms signal new demands**: bigrams such as *"haul crops"*,
   *"crew leaders"*, and *"agricultural products"* are rising in frequency,
   suggesting increased emphasis on logistics and supervision roles.

3. **TF-IDF by quarter** reveals seasonal and crop-specific language
   (e.g., terms distinctive to winter vs. summer quarters differ markedly).

4. **Verb-object pairs provide structured task profiles** per job order,
   enabling downstream analysis such as O\*NET skill mapping,
   occupational similarity scoring, or wage decomposition by task content.

---

# Computational Environment

```{r session}
sessionInfo()
```
